title: Anastasia's Mate: The Knight Locks the Escape Squares
lesson_text: In this simplified endgame position, White can finish immediately with the classic mating pattern called Anastasia's Mate. The key piece is the White knight on e7, which controls g8 and g6, the only escape squares for the Black king on h7. Black's own rook on g7 also hurts Black because it blocks the king from moving to g7. With the escape squares covered, White delivers the final blow with 1.Rh4#. The rook moves to the h-file and gives check along the open h-file, while the Black king has nowhere to run and no way to block or capture the rook. The important lesson is that checkmate does not always require many pieces. One knight can act like a shadow over the escape squares, while the rook delivers the finishing check. Here, the knight traps, the rook checks, and Black's own piece completes the mating net.

title: Geometry of Victory: The Rule of the Square
lesson_text: In this basic pawn endgame, White wins by using the Rule of the Square. The pawn on f4 wants to run to f8 and promote. To see if Black can stop it, imagine a square from the pawn to the promotion square. If the defending king can enter that square in time, the pawn can be caught. If not, the pawn will promote. Here, White moves first with 1. f5!. After the pawn advances, the square becomes smaller, and the Black king on a3 is still too far away to catch it. White does not need help from the king; the pawn simply runs forward. The key lesson is simple: before pushing a passed pawn, check whether the enemy king is inside or outside the square. If the king is outside the square and cannot enter in time, the pawn promotes.

title: The Rook Pawn Trap: Mastering the Corner Draw
lesson_text:
In this king-and-pawn endgame, Black can hold the draw because the remaining pawn is a rook pawn. White has an outside passer on the a-file, but Black's king has already reached the promotion corner on a8. This is the key defensive setup. Black's first move is forced: 1...Kb8. Even though the king steps out of the corner for a moment, Black is not losing. The idea is simple: stay close to the a8-square and return to the corner whenever White pushes the pawn. A natural line is:
1...Kb8 2.a5 Ka8 3.a6 Kb8 4.a7+ Ka8

Now White cannot make progress. The pawn wants to promote on a8, but Black's king occupies the promotion square. If White tries to force the king away with 5.Ka6, the position is stalemate because Black has no legal move and is not in check. This is the important beginner rule: a rook pawn can often draw if the defending king reaches the promotion corner. Even if the stronger side has a king and pawn, the attacker may not be able to drive the defender out without causing stalemate. Black's plan is not complicated: stay near the corner, avoid moving too far away, and let the rook pawn trap save the game.

title: The Corner Escape: Drawing with the Wrong Bishop
lesson_text: Do not resign yet  -  the game is not over. Even with a large material disadvantage, some endgames are still guaranteed draws. This position is a classic example of the wrong-colored bishop. White has a light-squared bishop on c8 and an h-pawn, but the promotion square h8 is a dark square. Since White's bishop can never control h8, Black can survive by keeping the king in or near the h8 corner. Black's goal is not to win the pawn, but to make sure the king cannot be driven away from the promotion square. If White pushes the pawn to h6 and then h7, Black simply stays on h8 whenever possible. Even if White's king reaches g6, Black is trapped but safe, because White has no bishop check on h8 and no way to force the king out. If White plays h7 and Black has no legal move, the position is stalemate. The important lesson is that a rook pawn plus the wrong-colored bishop cannot win if the defending king reaches the promotion corner. For Black, the drawing rule is simple: stay in the h8 corner and do not give up just because you are far behind in material.

title: The Promotion Strike: Removing the Last Defender
lesson_text: White can force mate, but the promotion must remove the defender first. The black rook on b8 is guarding the promotion zone, so the winning move is 1.axb8=Q!. This captures the rook and creates a queen at the same time. White should not play 1.a8=Q+?, because the rook on b8 remains and the position only draws after Black deals with the new queen. After 1.axb8=Q!, Black has only king moves, and White mates on the next move: if 1...Ka4, then 2.Qb4#; if 1...Ka6, then 2.Qb6#. The bishop on c5 is the key helper because it protects both b4 and b6. The lesson is simple: when a defender sits on the promotion route, capture it as part of the promotion and use your supporting piece to finish the mate.

title: Sniper's Path to Checkmate
lesson_text: White is winning, but only by using the bishop's long diagonal immediately. The black rook on a8 blocks the a7-pawn, and the precise move is 1.Bxa8!, capturing the blocker from h1. Tablebase confirms that other first moves let the win slip away. After 1.Bxa8, the pawn still cannot promote right away because White's bishop now occupies a8. The next step is to move the bishop off the promotion square while keeping control of the position, for example 1.Bxa8 Kg3 2.Bg2. Now the a-pawn is free to queen with a8=Q, and White has enough force to drive the black king to mate. The lesson is simple: first remove the piece on the promotion square, then clear that square for your pawn.

title: Endgame Essentials: Mastering the Vertical Retreat
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, Black draws by retreating correctly from in front of the passed pawn. The white king on e4 supports the pawn on e5, so Black cannot stay on e6 forever, but Black must not abandon the blockade. The clearest move is 1...Ke7!, stepping straight back while keeping the king on the pawn's file. The tablebase also confirms that 1...Kd7 and 1...Kf7 draw, but 1...Ke7 is the simplest beginner rule: stay in front of the pawn first. If White tries to outflank with 2.Kd5, Black must answer 2...Kd7, and if White tries 2.Kf5, Black must answer 2...Kf7. Those matching retreats keep the opposition and stop White's king from reaching the key squares. The moves ...Kd6 and ...Kf6 are not legal in the starting position because the e5-pawn controls those squares. The lesson is simple: retreat to the seventh rank, stay in front of the pawn, and only move sideways when it directly blocks the attacking king.

title: The Strategic Retreat: Holding the Draw with Distant Opposition
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, Black must avoid stepping away from the pawn's path. The tempting move 1...Kd4? loses because it lets White's king advance and support the e-pawn from the side. Instead, Black should retreat and stay in front of the pawn. Moves like 1...Kd6, 1...Ke6, and 1...Kf6 all hold the draw because Black keeps the blockade and prevents White's king from gaining decisive key squares. The important defensive rule is simple: when defending against a passed pawn, do not wander beside or behind it too early. Stay in front, keep opposition when possible, and force the attacking king to make no progress.

title: Two Steps Ahead: Winning with the King in Front
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White has already reached the dream setup from the endgame database: the king is two squares in front of a non-rook pawn. That distance is enough to win even if the defender is ready to chase. White can begin simply with 1.e5!, keeping the pawn protected by the king on e6 and taking away key squares from Black. If Black runs toward the pawn, White's king stays in front and escorts it forward. The important beginner rule is that the king must lead the pawn. When your king is two squares ahead of a center pawn, do not drift away looking for checks or tricks. Keep the king in front, push the pawn at the right moment, promote, and then use the new queen with the king to force mate.

title: The Power of the King: Mastering Vertical Opposition
lesson_text: In this fundamental king-and-pawn endgame, White wins by moving the king forward before pushing the pawn. The clearest beginner move is 1.Ke5!, taking direct opposition against the Black king on e7. Black must step aside, and White can use that tempo to reach the key squares in front of the e-pawn. The tablebase also shows that 1.Kd5! and 1.Kf5! win, but passive moves such as 1.Kd4?, 1.Kf4?, 1.Kd3?, and 1.Kf3? only draw because they let Black keep the blockade. After 1.Ke5 Kd7, White can continue 2.Kf6, then advance the pawn with e4 and e5 once the king has cleared the path. The lesson is simple: in king-and-pawn endings, the king must lead. First win access to the key squares, then push the pawn and promote.

title: King First: Taking Opposition on the Road to Promotion
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is winning, but only by using the king before the pawn. The key move is 1.Ke5!, taking the opposition against the Black king on e7. This move forces Black's king to give way, allowing White's king to step forward and take control of the important squares in front of the pawn. The pawn on e4 should not be pushed too early, because a premature pawn advance can let Black stand in front of it and hold the draw. White's goal is simple: place the king ahead of the pawn, drive the Black king away, and only then advance the pawn. Once White's king reaches the sixth rank with the pawn safely behind it, promotion becomes unstoppable. After the e-pawn promotes to a queen, White uses the king and queen together to force the Black king to the edge and deliver checkmate. The important lesson is that passed pawns usually need their king's help. Win the opposition first, then march the pawn forward.

title: King First: Winning the Opposition for the c-Pawn
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White wins by using the king before pushing the pawn. The correct move is 1.Kc5!, taking the opposition against the Black king on c7. This is the key step because White's king controls the important forward squares b6, c6, and d6, forcing the Black king to move aside. White should not rush with the c-pawn too early, because Black may get in front of it and hold the draw. Instead, White's king must lead the way, drive the Black king back, and clear the path for the pawn. Once the king has secured the promotion route, the c-pawn can safely advance toward c8. After promotion, White uses the new queen together with the king to force the Black king to the edge and deliver checkmate. The important lesson is simple: win the opposition first, let the king guard the pawn's path, then promote and finish the game.

title: The King's Shield: Escorting the Pawn to Promotion
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is winning, but the pawn cannot do the work alone. The correct idea is to lead with the king first. White should play 1.Kg6!, using the king as a shield to keep the Black king away from the f-pawn. This is similar to shouldering: the White king steps across the Black king's path and prevents it from reaching the pawn's promotion route. If White pushes the pawn too early, Black may get close enough to fight for the key squares. By moving the king first, White controls the important squares in front of the pawn and makes the advance safe. After the Black king is cut off, the f-pawn can march forward toward promotion. Once the pawn becomes a queen, White will have the winning material needed to force the Black king to the edge and deliver checkmate. The important lesson is simple: do not rush the pawn when the king can improve first. Use the king as a shield, escort the pawn, then promote and finish the game.

title: Shouldering the King Away: Clearing the Path to Promotion
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is winning, but only by using the king correctly before pushing the pawn. The key move is 1.Kb6!, a shouldering move. White's king steps in front of the Black king and cuts it off from the a-pawn's promotion path. Black wants to reach b4 or a5 to stop the pawn, but White's king on b6 controls the important approach squares and keeps the Black king away. After this, White can safely advance the pawn up the a-file. The pawn itself is not the final goal; it is the way to create a queen. Once the pawn promotes, White uses the king and queen together to force the Black king to the edge and deliver checkmate. The important lesson is that passed pawns often need help from the king first. Do not rush the pawn  -  shoulder the enemy king away, then promote and finish the game.

title: Connected Pawns: Push the Pawn That Is Free
lesson_text: In this pawn endgame, White's two connected passed pawns are strong, but only if they move together. Black's king on e3 attacks both pawns, so a quiet king move such as 1.Kg1? gives away the win. The simple winning rule is to push one pawn while the other pawn protects it. White can play 1.d5!, moving the d-pawn to a square guarded by the e4-pawn. If Black captures the e-pawn with 1...Kxe4, the d-pawn runs forward. If Black stays near the d-pawn, the e-pawn is still ready to advance. The tablebase also confirms that 1.e5! wins by the same connected-pawn idea. The lesson is simple: connected passed pawns should support each other. Do not move the king away when the pawns need a tempo; push one pawn, use the other as backup, promote, and finish with mate.

title: Dueling Wings: The Power of Split Passed Pawns
lesson_text: White wins by creating two passed pawns that are too far apart for Black's king to stop. The key move is 1.axb5!, removing Black's b-pawn and creating a queenside passer. This is important: the natural-looking 1.h5? only draws because Black can keep the queenside under control. After 1.axb5!, Black must choose which wing to chase. If the king moves toward the b-pawn, White pushes the h-pawn; if the king moves toward the h-pawn, White pushes the b-pawn. For example, after 1.axb5 Kf5, White can continue 2.b6, and the queenside pawn becomes the runner. The lesson is simple: first create the second passer, then use the distance between the pawns to overload the lone king. One pawn distracts the defender, and the other reaches promotion.

title: Avoiding Stalemate: Win with the King First
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is winning, but one careless pawn move can throw the win away. The tempting move is 1.g6?, but this allows Black to hide in the corner with 1...Kh8. After that, White may no longer be able to make progress without stalemating the Black king. The correct winning idea is to improve the king first with 1.Kf7!. White's king takes control of the key squares and keeps the Black king boxed in while still leaving it enough legal moves. This is the important difference: White must restrict the Black king without trapping it too early. Once the king is placed correctly, the g-pawn can advance at the right moment and promote safely. After promotion, White uses the new queen and king together to force checkmate. The lesson is simple but very important: when the enemy king is near the corner, do not rush the pawn. Use your king first, avoid stalemate, then promote and finish the game.

title: King Opposition: Taking the Winning Square
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White must use king opposition before trying to push the pawn. The position looks simple, but White must be exact. The only winning move is 1.Kb4!. White does not rush the pawn, and White does not step to c4 too early. The point of 1.Kb4! is to prepare the correct opposition at the right moment. If Black replies 1...Kc6, White now plays 2.Kc4!. This time, the move works because it is Black's turn to move. The kings face each other, and Black must give way. Once the Black king is forced back, White's king can take control of the key squares and help the pawn advance. A common mistake is 1.Kc4?. Even though it looks active, it only draws because Black can answer with 1...Kc6!, taking the opposition. Now White is the one who must move, and Black can hold the position. The lesson is simple: opposition is not just about moving your king close. It is about reaching the right square at the right time, so the opponent is the one forced to move away. Once White wins the opposition, the pawn can safely advance toward promotion, and promotion will eventually lead to mate.

title: Leading the Charge: The King's Path to Promotion
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White must be very accurate. Although the position looks simple, there is only one winning first move: 1.Kc2! White's pawn on c3 cannot win by running alone. If White pushes too early or moves the king to the wrong square, Black can reach the drawing zone and use opposition to hold the draw. The correct idea is to bring the king to c2 first. From there, White can guide the pawn forward while fighting for the key squares in front of the pawn. Once White wins the opposition at the right moment, the pawn can advance safely toward promotion. This lesson is best taught after a basic opposition lesson, because the winning method depends on understanding how kings fight for access to important squares.

title: Beginner Lucena Bridge: Block the Rook and Promote to Mate
lesson_text: This is a simplified Lucena Bridge position where White's pawn is already very close to promotion. The pawn on d7 looks ready to queen, but White must not rush with 1.d8=Q? because the black rook on d1 would capture the new queen. The correct idea is to build a small bridge first with 1.Rd5!. This move places the rook between the black rook and the promotion square. Now the black rook can no longer stop the pawn from reaching d8. If Black captures with 1...Rxd5+, White simply replies 2.Kxd5, and the d-pawn will promote. If Black does not capture, White can promote safely with d8=Q, reaching an easily winning position that will soon lead to mate. This lesson introduces the Lucena Bridge in its simplest form: before promoting, use the rook to block the defender's rook. Once the bridge is built, the pawn can queen safely and White's extra queen will finish the game.

title: Standing Your Ground: The Power of Opposition
lesson_text: In this locked pawn ending, the tablebase confirms that the position is drawn. White does not have to find a single only move, but 1.Ke3 is the clearest teaching move because it keeps the king close to the blocked d-pawns and meets Black's king on the same file. The important point is not to chase a win. With pawns fixed on d4 and d5, neither side can create a passed pawn without letting the enemy king reach the stopping squares. After 1.Ke3, if Black tries to improve with moves such as ...Kf5 and ...Ke4, White can retreat calmly and stay in front of the pawn structure. Even if Black eventually wins the d4-pawn, White's king remains close enough to blockade the remaining d-pawn. The lesson is that opposition in locked pawn endings is often about holding the correct zone, not forcing the opponent backward immediately. Stay near the pawn chain, keep access to the blockade squares, and the draw is secure.

title: The Invisible Barrier: Holding the Draw with Distant Opposition
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, Black draws by keeping control of the squares in front of White's e-pawn. The tablebase confirms that every legal Black first move draws, so 1...Kf7 is not the only answer. The most direct drawing move is 1...Ke5, stepping in front of the pawn at once, while 1...Ke7, 1...Kd7, and 1...Kf7 also draw by keeping the king close enough to take opposition later. If Black chooses the distant-opposition plan with 1...Kf7 and White improves with 2.Ke3, Black should return toward the pawn with 2...Ke7 or another close defensive move. What Black must avoid later is drifting too far away from the e-file, because that can let White's king win the key squares. The lesson is simple: when defending against a lone center pawn, stay in front of it when possible, mirror the attacking king when needed, and do not let the stronger king lead the pawn from the front.

title: The Long-Distance Mirror: Mastering Distant Opposition
lesson_text: In this sparse king-and-pawn endgame, White has an extra c-pawn, but the position is already drawn. Black's king is directly in front of the pawn and far enough ahead to hold the key squares. With the kings on c1 and c7 and White to move, Black has the distant opposition: White cannot force the king to c6, b6, or d6 under favorable conditions. The tablebase confirms that every legal White first move draws, including 1.Kc2 and 1.c4. If White tries 1.Kc2, Black can keep the blockade with a move such as 1...Kc6 or stay nearby with 1...Kb6. If White pushes with c4 and c5, Black remains close enough to stop the pawn before it promotes. The defensive lesson is simple: against a lone center pawn, keep the king in front of the pawn, mirror the attacking king when needed, and never allow the stronger king to take the critical squares in front of the pawn. If those squares stay controlled, the extra pawn cannot win.

title: The Locked Blockade: Extra Pawn Still Draws
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is a pawn up, but the position is still drawn. The b-pawns are locked on b4 and b5, and Black's king on a6 controls the queenside entry points. White cannot force the king through, and the extra a-pawn is not enough to create a winning zugzwang. The tablebase confirms that every legal White move holds the draw, including 1.a4, 1.Ka1, 1.Kb1, 1.Kb2, and 1.Kb3. The important lesson is not to overestimate a reserve tempo. Playing a4 changes the pawn structure, but it does not break Black's blockade or create a passed pawn that can queen by force. Black's defensive rule is simple: keep the king near the locked pawns, stay ready to meet White's king activity, and do not abandon the b5 blockade. When the defender controls the entry squares and the pawn chain cannot be opened, even an extra pawn may not be enough to win.

title: The Rook-Pawn Fortress: Holding the Blockade
lesson_text: In this four-piece pawn ending, Black is the side holding the draw. The pawns on a2 and a3 are locked, and because the fight is confined to the rook-pawn file, White cannot use normal opposition to force a winning breakthrough. Black does not need to win the a2-pawn or calculate a delicate race; the defensive task is simply to keep the king near the a-file and b-file. From the starting position, every legal Black king move holds, including 1...Ka6, 1...Kb6, 1...Kb7, 1...Ka8, and 1...Kb8. If White approaches the pawn, Black stays close enough to the rook-pawn corner to make any capture harmless. Even if the locked pawns eventually disappear, White is left with the familiar rook-pawn draw because Black's king can reach the promotion corner. The lesson is simple: in rook-pawn endings, the defender often holds by staying near the corner, not by winning the opposition.

title: The Unbreakable Knight: Defensive Coordination
lesson_text: In this rook-versus-knight ending, Black's goal is to hold the draw by keeping the king and knight coordinated. The tablebase confirms that the position is drawn, but Black must avoid putting the knight where the rook can win it for free. The knight on f2 is attacked by White's king, yet it is safe because the black king on f3 protects it. Good drawing moves include 1...Nd1, 1...Nd3, 1...Ne4, 1...Ng4, 1...Ke3, and 1...Kg3. The move 1...Ne4 is especially natural: the knight becomes active in the center, and if White plays Rxe4, Black's king recaptures and the game is immediately drawn. Black must avoid 1...Nh1? and 1...Nh3?, because White can capture the knight with the rook and then win with rook against bare king. The key lesson is simple: in rook versus knight defense, do not let the knight become loose. Keep it protected, trade it if the rook captures it, and the draw is secure.

title: Distant Passed Pawns: A Path to Promotion and Mate
lesson_text: White wins because the two passed pawns are too far apart for Black's king to stop both. The fastest tablebase move is 1.h5!, immediately asking Black which side to chase. If Black moves toward the kingside, White pushes the a-pawn; if Black turns toward the queenside, the h-pawn runs. For example, 1.h5 Kf5 2.a5 Kg5 3.a6 Kxh5 4.a7, and the a-pawn promotes. The original plan 1.a5! also wins by the same logic, but it is not the only method. The important lesson is the overload: a lone king can sometimes catch one passed pawn, but it cannot cover two widely separated runners. Once one pawn promotes, White uses the new queen and king to force mate.

title: Coordinating King and Queen to Force Mate
lesson_text: White is winning, but the finish requires exact king-and-queen coordination because several natural queen moves only draw. The fastest move is 1.Kg6!, using the king to close the h-file escape squares and forcing Black into rook checks or passive defense. The tablebase main line is 1...Rg7+ 2.Kh6 Rf7 3.Qa8+! Rf8 4.Qxf8#, with the queen forcing the rook onto f8 and then capturing it as mate. White can also meet 2...Rg6+ accurately, but the capture matters: 3.Kxg6! wins, while 3.Qxg6?? is stalemate because the black king on h8 has no legal move. The lesson is that a dominant king can neutralize the checking rook, but the queen must deliver forcing checks rather than careless captures. The final mate comes from removing the rook on f8, not from an immediate queen move to e8.

title: Simplifying for the Crown: Forcing the Mate
lesson_text: Black is winning, and the cleanest conversion is to remove White's rook immediately with 1...Rxb8!. This is not a rook trade: White's king on f2 is much too far away to recapture, so Black wins the loose rook outright and keeps both the rook and the dangerous a-pawn. The tablebase shows that this is the fastest route to mate, while some careless rook moves even let the win slip away. After 1...Rxb8, Black's plan is simple: use the rook to support the a-pawn and force promotion. A typical line is 1...Rxb8 2.Ke3 Rb4 3.Kd2 a2 4.Kc1 a1=Q+. Once the pawn becomes a queen, Black coordinates queen, rook, and king to finish the mate. The lesson is that before racing a passed pawn, first remove the defender when it is tactically available. Winning White's rook makes promotion and mate straightforward.

title: The Promotion Check: Winning the Rook
lesson_text: White is winning, but the key is not a sacrifice. The precise move is 1.a8=Q+!, promoting with check while the black rook remains trapped on a1. Black cannot answer with Rxa8 because the black king on a6 blocks the rook's path up the a-file, so the only legal reply is 1...Kb5. Then 2.Qxa1 removes the rook outright and leaves White with queen, bishop, king, and pawn against a lone king. The tablebase also shows why accuracy matters: 1.a8=R+ and 1.a8=B still win more slowly, but 1.a8=N? and ordinary waiting moves only draw. The lesson is to promote with maximum force when the new queen arrives with tempo, then collect the defender before converting the forced mate.

title: The Road to Mate: Removing the Final Defender
lesson_text: White is winning, but only by removing Black's last active defender immediately. The bishop on c5 is attacking the White king and also helps Black interfere with the a-pawn, so the precise first move is 1.Kxc5!. Tablebase confirms that every other White king move lets the win slip away. After the bishop disappears, Black can still choose between 1...Kb7 and 1...Kxa7, but neither defense is enough. If the a-pawn falls, White's connected g- and h-pawns become the real winning force. A typical line is 1.Kxc5 Kb7 2.g4 Kxa7 3.g5 Kb7 4.g6 Kc7 5.g7, and the g-pawn promotes. Once White has a queen, the king and queen coordinate to drive the black king to the edge and deliver mate. The lesson is simple: when one defender is holding the position together, remove it before starting the pawn race.

title: The King's Preemptive Shift
lesson_text: This position is a tablebase draw, but White must avoid letting the rook and king win the bishop. The practical move is 1.Kc2!, stepping closer to the black king while keeping the bishop safely placed on c3. This is not the only drawing move: immediate promotion with 1.a8=Q+ also draws after 1...Rxa8, and several bishop moves such as 1.Be1 or 1.Bd2 hold as well. The moves to avoid are 1.Bb2?, 1.Ba1?, and 1.Bh8?, because they allow Black to win with accurate rook play or by capturing the loose bishop. After 1.Kc2, Black can still check with 1...Rg2+ or keep the rook on the back rank, but White's king and bishop remain coordinated enough to hold. The lesson is that when a rook controls the promotion square, the defender does not need to force promotion immediately; the priority is preserving piece coordination and avoiding a loose bishop.

title: Mastering the Safe Corner: Rook vs. Bishop Draw
lesson_text: In this rook-versus-bishop endgame, Black draws by using the safe corner. The bishop is a dark-squared bishop, so the king belongs in the light corner on a8. White's king on a6 and rook on h7 have boxed the king in, but that is exactly why the corner is safe: White cannot force mate as long as Black keeps a legal bishop move and does not let the bishop be won. The immediate task is to move the bishop from b8 to a square where the rook cannot capture it. The tablebase drawing moves are 1...Bg3!, 1...Bf4!, 1...Be5!, and 1...Bd6!. 1...Bg3! is the clearest example because the bishop moves far away, stays mobile on the long diagonal, and gives Black waiting moves while the king remains sheltered on a8. Black must avoid 1...Bc7?, 1...Bh2?, and 1...Ba7?, because White can simply capture with Rxc7, Rxh2, or Rxa7+, leaving a winning rook-versus-bare-king ending. The defensive rule is simple: keep the king in the corner opposite the bishop's color, and keep the bishop on a mobile square where the rook cannot take it. If those two conditions hold, White has no forced win.

title: Simplify to Draw: Removing the Bishop
lesson_text: White is not winning this position, but the advanced pawn on d7 gives enough counterplay to hold the draw if White avoids tactical mistakes. The cleanest drawing move is 1.Kxe5!, using the king to remove Black's bishop and simplify into a rook-and-pawn ending. This is not a breakthrough to promotion; after accurate Black defense, moves such as 1...Rb6 or 1...Rb1 keep the d-pawn under control. The point for White is that the dangerous pawn prevents Black from playing for more. White must not get careless with the rook: 1.Rb7?, 1.Rc7?, or 1.Ra8? allow Black to win material and turn the draw into a loss. The lesson is practical: when a win is unavailable, simplify into the drawn ending and keep the passed pawn as a shield against the opponent's activity.

title: The Distant Passer's Diversion
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White can hold the draw by using the a-pawn as an outside diversion. Black has a kingside majority with pawns on g6 and h5 against White's f-pawn, so passive play lets Black centralize the king and roll the kingside pawns. The practical drawing resource is 1.a5!. This creates a distant passer that Black cannot ignore. If Black stays on the kingside, White keeps pushing with a6 and threatens promotion. If Black's king turns toward the a-pawn, White gains time to keep the king active around d2, d3, or d4 and remain close enough to stop Black's kingside pawns. White should not drift away with moves like 1.Kb2?, 1.Kc2?, or 1.Kb3?, and should not start kingside play with 1.f3? or 1.f4?, because those moves let Black win the race. The defensive rule is simple: when the enemy has a compact pawn majority, create the outside passer first. A distant pawn threat can pull the enemy king away and give your king the time it needs to hold the other wing.

title: The King's Mirror: Precision in the Pawn-Locked Draw
lesson_text: This pawn-locked endgame is a tablebase draw, but White still has to keep the king in the correct defensive zone. The active teaching move is 1.Ke4!, stepping into the center and challenging Black to find the only drawing reply. If Black drifts with 1...Kc6, 1...Kc7, 1...Kd7, or 1...Ke7, White wins by outflanking the king and attacking the fixed pawns. Black's accurate answer is 1...Ke6, taking the vertical opposition; then White must mirror precisely with 2.Kf4! to hold the barrier. The tablebase also shows that quieter first moves such as 1.Kd2 and 1.Ke2 draw, while 1.Ke3?, 1.Kc3?, and 1.Kc2? lose by allowing Black to seize the opposition and penetrate. The lesson is not that White has only one drawing move, but that in locked pawn structures the king must stay on squares that preserve opposition and deny the opponent an outflanking route.

title: Mastering the Square: Drawing Against the Lone Passer
lesson_text: In this pawn ending, White can hold the draw, but the king must move toward the a-pawn immediately. The tablebase shows that 1.Kc3! is a clear drawing move, and 1.Kc2 also draws; the losing moves are the ones that move away from the outside passer or start counterplay too soon. After 1.Kc3!, Black's king is boxed in: it cannot use b3 or b4 to escort the pawn, and if it steps to a2, White keeps the blockade and remains close enough to stop the a-pawn. White should not play 1.Kc4?, 1.Kd4?, 1.Ke4?, 1.Kd2?, 1.Ke2?, or 1.e4?, because those moves give Black time to free the a-pawn and win the race. The defensive lesson is to stop the passer first. Enter the square, block the supporting king, and only then think about your own pawn.

title: Mastering the Shoulder: Drawing the Pawn Endgame
lesson_text:
In this pawn ending, White draws by fixing the kingside structure first and then using the king as a barrier. A clean practical move is 1.h4!, which stops Black from choosing the best moment to advance the h-pawn and gives White a clear defensive plan.

Once the pawn structure is fixed, White's king must come across to the kingside. The key idea is shouldering: White wants to meet Black's king from the side and claim the critical f4 square. From there, the white king acts as a physical wall, cutting the black king off from the h-pawn's promotion path.

White is not trying to win the pawn immediately. White is trying to reach the right geometry. If Black eventually wins the h4-pawn with the king, the draw still holds as long as White reaches the shouldering setup in time.

The lesson is simple but important: in pawn endings, drawing often depends less on speed than on structure and king placement. Here, fixing the pawn with h4 and then shouldering from f4 is enough to hold the game.

title: The Philidor Barrier: Holding the Sixth-Rank Draw
lesson_text: In this rook-and-pawn endgame, Black can hold the draw by using the Philidor defensive setup. White has an extra pawn on f5 and an active king on e5, but Black's rook on h6 is already doing the most important defensive job: creating a sixth-rank barrier. Since White's rook on a8 is giving check, Black must answer accurately with 1...Kf7!, stepping in front of the passed pawn. Black should not play 1...Ke7?, because White can reply 2.f6+!, driving the king away and destroying the defensive setup. The key idea is to keep the king in front of the pawn while the rook prevents the White king from advancing to e6 or f6. As long as White's pawn stays on f5, Black simply maintains the barrier and waits. If White finally pushes f6 without check, the pawn no longer shields the White king on the fifth rank, so Black can switch plans with ...Rh5+!, checking from the side. After that, the White king cannot escape the repeated rook checks without losing progress. The important lesson is simple: in the Philidor draw, first build the barrier, wait for the pawn to advance, then check from the side once the attacking king loses its cover.

title: The Reti Maneuver: Drawing by Taking the Magic Diagonal
lesson_text: In this classic endgame study, White appears to be lost. Black's h-pawn is racing toward promotion, while White's c-pawn looks too far away to save because the Black king is close enough to attack it. The drawing idea is the famous Reti maneuver: 1.Kg7!. Instead of running straight toward one pawn, White's king takes a diagonal path that keeps two goals alive at the same time. After 1...h4, White continues with 2.Kf6!. Now Black has a difficult choice. If Black uses the king to chase the c-pawn, White's king can turn back toward the h-pawn and arrive in time to stop it. But if Black keeps pushing the h-pawn, White's king moves closer to the c-pawn and helps it promote. For example, after 2...h3, White can play 3.Ke7!, and the c-pawn is ready to run. The lesson is that king geometry matters. A diagonal king move can cover two distant goals at once: stopping the enemy pawn and supporting your own. This is why the shortest winning or drawing path in chess is not always a straight line.

title: The Shouldering Shield: Marching to Mate
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White has only two legal moves, but only one of them wins. The correct move is 1.Kg6!, using the king to shoulder Black's king away from the h-pawn. This move places the White king between the Black king and the pawn's promotion path. Black wants to get close enough to stop the h-pawn or trap White near the corner, but 1.Kg6! cuts off the important approach squares and gives the pawn the support it needs. The natural-looking 1.Kh6? is only a draw, because it does not block Black's king in the right way. Black can stay close enough to the h-pawn and prevent White from making winning progress. The key lesson is that rook pawns are especially tricky: one wrong king move can turn a win into a draw. White must not simply move toward the corner. White must shoulder the defender away with 1.Kg6!, then escort the h-pawn to promotion and finish the game with king and queen against king.

title: The Hidden Tempo: Forcing Zugzwang in Pawn Endgames
lesson_text: White is winning because the h-pawn gives a reserve tempo in an otherwise tense opposition fight. The clean thematic move is 1.h4!, keeping the white king on e3 while handing the move to Black. The tablebase also shows that active king moves such as 1.Kd4 and 1.Kf4 win, but 1.h4! best illustrates the hidden tempo. White must not rush with 1.e5?, because Black can answer 1...Kxe5 and the position is drawn. After 1.h4!, Black is in zugzwang: if the king steps back or sideways, White's king gains access to the key squares; if Black moves toward the e-pawn with 1...Ke5, White can switch plans and run the h-pawn with 2.h5. The lesson is that a reserve tempo is powerful because it preserves the main pawn's flexibility while forcing the defender to declare a plan first.

title: Triangulation: Forcing Zugzwang Before the c6 Breakthrough
lesson_text:
In this king-and-pawn endgame, White has a dangerous queenside pawn pair, but the win is not just about pushing immediately. The key idea is triangulation: White uses the king to lose a move and force Black into zugzwang.
Black's king on d7 is tied to the defense. If Black could simply pass, the blockade might hold. But kings cannot pass, so White can maneuver with the king to give the move back to Black at the worst possible moment. White's goal is to keep the king active while forcing Black away from the critical squares. Once Black is placed in zugzwang, the c-pawn advance becomes decisive. The move c6+ then breaks the blockade: if Black captures with ...bxc6, White's b-pawn becomes a passed pawn; if Black does not capture, the c-pawn advances and Black's position collapses. The important lesson is that the breakthrough works best after the defender has been forced into the wrong move. Do not rush the pawn break blindly. First use triangulation to control the move order, then strike with c6 when Black can no longer hold the blockade. Main teaching point: Triangulation is not just 'moving around'. It is a way to give the move to the opponent and force zugzwang, making a pawn breakthrough work at the right moment.

title: Timing the Breakthrough: The H-Pawn Reserve Tempo
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn endgame, White is winning, but the e-pawn must not be pushed too soon. The natural 1.e5? only draws, because Black can capture on e5 or take up a solid blockade. The cleanest winning move is 1.h4!, using the h-pawn as a reserve tempo while keeping the e-pawn flexible. White gives Black the move and forces the defending king to choose a square before White commits the central pawn. A typical winning line begins 1.h4 Ke5 2.Kd3 Kf4 3.Kd4. Now White's king has improved, and the e-pawn can advance with support. After 3...Kg4 4.e5 Kf5 5.h5, Black cannot stop both pawns: if the king chases the e-pawn, the h-pawn runs; if the king chases the h-pawn, the e-pawn advances. The lesson is that a reserve tempo is not just any spare pawn move. It must be used before the main pawn loses flexibility, so the defender is forced to give ground at the critical moment.

title: The Reserve Tempo: Mastering Zugzwang in Pawn Endgames
lesson_text: In this five-piece pawn ending, White is winning because the pawn on a2 gives a spare move at exactly the right moment. The cleanest thematic move is 1.a3!, using the reserve tempo to pass the move to Black while the White king on b5 and pawn on c6 keep the Black king boxed in. Black has no useful waiting move: b7 is controlled by the c6-pawn, and a6 and b6 are controlled by the White king, so the king must step to a8 or b8. Then White can centralize with 2.Kc5 or 2.Kc4 and start squeezing the c7 blockade. A typical plan is 1.a3! Ka8 2.Kc5 Ka7 3.Kd5 Kb6 4.a4!, keeping one more pawn lever ready for a5+. If Black is pulled away after a5+, White can capture on c7 and promote the c-pawn. The king move 1.Ka5! also wins, but it is not the clearest reserve-tempo lesson. The drawing mistakes are 1.Ka4? and 1.Kb4?, which let Black answer with ...Ka6 or ...Kb6 and rebuild the blockade. The key rule is simple: a reserve tempo wins only when the king and advanced pawn already control the defender's active squares.

title: The King's Maneuver: Decoys and Shouldering
lesson_text: In this technical pawn endgame, White wins by combining a decoy on the edge with active king play in the center. The a6-pawn ties Black's king to the queenside: if Black ignores it, the pawn can advance and force promotion or a favorable simplification; if Black captures it, White gains the time needed to bring the king forward. A clean plan begins with 1.Ke3!, heading for d4, e5, and e6. After 1.Ke3 Kxa6 2.Kd4 Kb7 3.Ke5 Kc7 4.Ke6!, White uses shouldering to cut off the Black king from the central approach squares. Black cannot hold the c6 blockade forever, and White can continue with Kd6 and Kxc6. Once the c6-pawn falls, White's c-pawn advances with king support and promotion becomes decisive. The lesson is that an outside passer does more than race to queen: it can pull the defender away while your king wins the critical squares.

title: The Distant Diversion: Mastering the Passed Pawn
lesson_text: White is winning, and the thematic move is 1.d5!, creating a distant passed pawn that pulls Black's king away from the queenside. The tablebase also shows that some king moves win, but 1.d5! is the cleanest lesson because it immediately gives Black two problems: stop the d-pawn and keep the b5-pawn defended. After 1.d5 Kb6, White must not rush with 2.d6?, which only draws. Instead, White improves the king with 2.Kc3! or 2.Kc2!, preparing to support the d-pawn while threatening to win the b5-pawn. A typical plan is 1.d5 Kb6 2.Kc3 Kc7 3.Kd4 Kd6 4.Ke4, when White's king has joined the fight and Black can no longer hold both fronts. The lesson is that a passed pawn is often strongest as a diversion first. Use it to pull the defender out of position, then bring your king in to win the second weakness and promote.

title: The Magnet Strategy: Mastering the Outside Passer
lesson_text: In this technical pawn endgame, White wins because the outside passed pawn on a6 acts like a magnet. Black's king must spend time stopping or capturing it, and that gives White's king time to reach the center. The accurate first move is 1.Ke3!, not an immediate pawn push. If White rushes with 1.c4?, Black draws by taking the c-pawn's key squares. After 1.Ke3!, Black may capture the decoy with 1...Kxa6, but White continues 2.Kd4 and 3.Kd5, reaching a dominant position in front of the c-pawn. From there the White king controls the critical squares, the c-pawn can advance safely, and promotion becomes unavoidable. The lesson is that an outside passer does not always promote itself. Often its real job is to pull the defending king away while your king wins the race to the decisive squares.

title: The Art of the Outflank: Breaking the Blockade
lesson_text:
In this king-and-pawn endgame, White has a winning position because Black cannot hold the opposition forever. The pawns on e5 and e6 block each other, so the battle is decided by the kings. White should begin with the outflanking move 1.Kc5!. This steps to the side and forces Black to choose where to retreat. If Black tries to stay near the pawn with 1...Kd7, White continues with 2.Kb6!, pushing the black king farther away from the defense of the e6-pawn. The important idea is that Black cannot keep the opposition and defend the pawn at the same time. Once White's king reaches the queenside and then returns through c6 or d6, Black is forced back. White will eventually win the pawn on e6, leaving a winning king-and-pawn position. A sample winning plan is:
1.Kc5 Kd7 2.Kb6 Kc8 3.Kc6 Kd8 4.Kd6
Now Black is in trouble. The black king must give way, and White will capture the e6-pawn. After that, White's remaining e-pawn can advance with king support and eventually promote.
The key lesson: when the defender blocks your pawn directly, do not push too early. Use your king to outflank the defender, force the king away, and only then win the blocked pawn.

title: The Lucena Bridge: Promoting to Deliver Mate
lesson_text: In this rook-and-pawn endgame, White has reached the famous Lucena position. The pawn on d7 is only one step from promotion, but White's own king is blocking the promotion square. If the king runs away too early without protection, Black's rook can give repeated checks and force the White king back. The winning first move is 1.Rf4!. This is the key Lucena idea: White prepares to build a bridge. The rook moves to the fourth rank so it can later block Black's checking rook and give the White king shelter. A typical line is 1.Rf4! Rc1 2.Ke7 Re1+ 3.Kd6 Rd1+ 4.Ke6 Re1+ 5.Kd5 Rd1+ 6.Rd4!. Now the bridge is complete. White's rook blocks the check, and Black cannot keep checking forever. If Black captures the rook, White's king recaptures and the d-pawn promotes. If Black avoids the exchange, White will promote the pawn anyway. The important lesson is simple: do not rush the king out without protection. First build the bridge with the rook, then walk the king away from the promotion square, block the checks, and finally promote the pawn. Since this is a mate goal lesson, the promotion is not the final goal by itself; it is the step that gives White enough force to deliver mate. When your pawn is on the seventh rank and your king is in front of it, remember the Lucena rule: build the bridge before promoting.

title: Punishing the Philidor Mistake: Push with Check
lesson_text: In this rook-and-pawn endgame, Black has already made the critical mistake by playing the king to e7 instead of f7. This allows White to win with the forcing move 2.f6+!. The check is the key difference. In the correct Philidor defense, Black wants to keep the king in front of the pawn and the rook on the sixth rank, but here the pawn advance comes with tempo and attacks the Black king. If Black tries 2...Rxf6, White simply plays 3.Kxf6+ and wins the rook. If the Black king moves away, White keeps a protected pawn on f6 and the Philidor barrier is gone. White can then support the pawn with the king and rook until promotion becomes unavoidable. The important lesson is that small king-placement details decide rook-and-pawn endgames. In the previous position, 1...Kf7! draws, but 1...Ke7? loses because it allows f6+ with check.

title: The Frozen King: Winning with a Second Front
lesson_text: White is winning, but only by creating the second front immediately. The a7-pawn ties Black's king to the corner, but it is not enough by itself: if White plays slowly or pushes 1.c4?, the position only draws. The precise first move is 1.cxd4!, removing Black's d-pawn and creating a new passed pawn on d4. Now Black cannot deal with both threats. If Black captures the a-pawn with 1...Kxa7, White brings the king forward with 2.Ke3, 3.Ke4, and 4.Ke5, then escorts the d-pawn. If Black stays near a8, the d-pawn and White king still advance while the a-pawn keeps Black tied down. The lesson is that a far-advanced pawn can freeze the defender long enough to open a second front, but the breakthrough must come at once.

title: King First: Escorting the g-Pawn
lesson_text: In this king-and-pawn ending, White wins only by leading with the king. The correct first move is 1.Kh4!, stepping to the side of the pawn and preparing to take the key squares in front of it. The tempting 1.g5? draws, because the pawn runs ahead before the king has secured the promotion path. After 1.Kh4!, White must still be precise: if Black heads toward the pawn with 1...Kf6, White answers 2.Kh5!, keeping the king in front and forcing the defender back. If Black stays farther away, White can use Kg5, Kh5, or then g5 at the right moment, always making sure the king leads the pawn. Once the g-pawn promotes, the new queen and king have enough force to deliver mate. The lesson is simple: in king-and-pawn endings, do not push first when your king can take the winning escort square.

title: The Geometry of Distant Opposition
lesson_text: White can win this bare king-and-pawn ending, but only by using the king before the pawn. The winning first moves are 1.Kb2! and the symmetric 1.Kd2!, taking distant opposition against the Black king on c8. The immediate pawn moves 1.c3? and 1.c4? only draw because Black can stay in front of the pawn. After 1.Kb2!, Black must give ground, and White can walk the king forward before advancing the c-pawn. A typical plan is 1.Kb2 Kb7 2.Kb3 Kb6 3.Kc4 Kc6 4.c3, with White's king already close enough to win the key squares. Once White's king reaches the sixth rank and the pawn advances safely, promotion follows. The lesson is that distant opposition is not just a counting trick; it is a way to make the defender move first so your king can lead the pawn.

title: The Unbreakable Corner: Holding the Blockade
lesson_text: Black to move holds the draw despite White's advanced king and two connected-looking pawns. The fortress works because the black king controls the a-pawn's promotion corner while the pawn on c7 blocks White's c-pawn. Black does not need to win anything immediately; the drawing rule is to keep the king on the a-file or b-file and never abandon the blockade. From the starting position, both 1...Ka7 and 1...Kb8 draw. If White pushes 2.a7, Black simply captures with Kxa7. If White tries to improve with Kc5, Ka5, or Kb4, Black stays near a7/b8 and White has no triangulation that forces the king away. The lesson is that space and extra pawns are not enough when the defender controls the rook-pawn corner and physically blocks the other passer.

title: Second-Rank Retreat: A Safe Rook Draw
lesson_text: This rook-and-pawn ending is drawn, so White's task is not to press for a win but to avoid drifting into a lost rook ending. The tablebase shows that every legal White move holds, and 1.Rh2 is a clean practical choice. By retreating to the second rank, the rook stays far from the Black king, keeps lateral checking distance, and remains ready to support the f-pawn without allowing easy trades. White should keep the king near the pawn and avoid overextending unless the rook is active enough to give checks from the side or behind. After 1.Rh2, Black still needs accurate defense, with moves such as 1...Rd4 or king moves toward d6, d7, or d8 holding the draw. The lesson is modest but important: in rook endings, a safe rook placement and checking distance are often more valuable than trying to force promotion immediately.

title: The Iron Fortress: Neutralizing the Queen
lesson_text: White is down a queen, but the position is drawn because Black's king is trapped. The bishop on f7 controls g8, the pawn on g6 controls h7, and the rook gives White enough checking and harassment power to keep the queen from organizing a win. A clean drawing move is 1.Re3!, attacking the queen along the third rank. Black cannot simply take the rook: after 1...Qxe3+? 2.Kxe3, White wins with the bishop and advanced g-pawn. White's practical rule is to keep the fortress coordinated: preserve the bishop-and-pawn cage around the Black king, keep the White king safe, and use the rook actively to check or chase the queen. Careless moves such as 1.Ba2?, 1.Bb3?, 1.Bg8?, or king retreats like 1.Ke1? and 1.Kg1? lose because they break the coordination that makes the fortress work.

title: The Vancura Defense: Checking from the Side
lesson_text: This rook-and-pawn ending is a textbook Vancura defense. White has a rook pawn on a6 and the rook in front of it on a8, but Black can still draw because the black rook attacks from the side and the black king is near the safe corner. The clearest move is 1...Rf4+!, checking the White king before it can hide behind the pawn. If White's king moves toward the pawn, Black keeps checking along the f-file. If White pushes 2.a7, Black changes jobs with 2...Ra6!, moving behind the pawn and stopping promotion. The key defensive lesson is that the rook must keep side contact with the pawn from a distance. Do not passively wait, and do not let the attacking king shelter on b6 or b7. Check from the side, return behind the pawn when it reaches the seventh rank, and the rook-pawn ending stays drawn.

title: King and Queen Coordination in the Queen vs. Rook Ending
lesson_text: White is winning, and the tablebase confirms that 1.Kb6! is the fastest move. The king steps closer to the trapped black king and leaves Black with no useful rook-check defense. After the toughest reply 1...Kc8, White plays 2.Qa6+!, forcing the king toward d7 or d8 and lining up the queen with the loose rook on c4. The key tactical point is not a long maneuver through e2 and e4, but the immediate conversion: 2...Kd7 3.Qxc4 wins the rook. From there White has queen and king against a lone king, and the tablebase line finishes efficiently with 3...Ke7 4.Qf4 Ke6 5.Kc6 Ke7 6.Qf5 Ke8 7.Kd6 Kd8 8.Qd7#. The lesson is that queen-versus-rook technique often turns on coordination before the rook falls: use the king to restrict the opposing king, force it onto a bad square with check, then take the exposed rook.

title: The King's Bodycheck: Securing the Promotion for Mate
lesson_text: White is winning, but the knight means the promotion must be handled accurately. The tablebase gives two fastest first moves: 1.a7! and 1.Kb5!. The bodycheck plan starts with 1.Kb5!, bringing the king closer to the promotion square and restricting Black's king before the pawn runs. A typical line is 1.Kb5 Nd3 2.a7 Kb2 3.a8=Q, when White has promoted while keeping the king active enough to help the queen deal with the knight. The original idea of playing 1.a7 first also wins, but after 1...Nd3 the fastest continuation is 2.a8=Q, not a preparatory king move. Once White has the queen, the job is not finished instantly: White must coordinate queen and king to restrict the knight, win it if possible, and force mate. The lesson is that promotion against a knight is not just a race. King placement matters because it reduces counterplay during the queen-versus-knight conversion.

title: The Narrow Gate: Winning with the Only Entry Square
lesson_text: In this five-piece pawn ending, White is winning, but only with the precise first move 1.Ka5!. This is not a triangulation position. White cannot move directly to b5 because the black pawn on c6 controls that square, and every other legal king move lets the win slip away. The point of 1.Ka5! is to use the a6-pawn and the White king together to trap Black's king. From a7, Black has only 1...Ka8 or 1...Kb8. Then White plays 2.Kb6!, reaching the key square from the side and bypassing the c6-pawn's control of b5. Once the king arrives on b6, Black cannot save the c6-pawn or stop White from creating a passed pawn. A typical finish is 1.Ka5! Kb8 2.Kb6 Ka8 3.Kxc6, after which White's remaining pawns decide. The lesson is that some pawn endings are won by finding the only legal path into the position, not by losing a tempo.

title: The Shielding Maneuver: Securing the Draw by Interposition
lesson_text:
White is only drawing here if the defense is exact. Black threatens the immediate mating idea ...Rh1#, so the only drawing move is 1.Re3!. This rook lift places the rook on the third rank, where it shields the king and interferes with Black's mating net.

White is not trying to save the rook. White is using it to force Black into a specific setup. If Black tries 1...Rh1+?, White has 2.Rh3!, blocking the check and keeping the h-pawn alive. Black's best practical reply is 1...Kxe3, accepting the rook and hoping to win with rook against pawn.

Now comes the real point of the lesson: White must play 2.Kh5!. The natural push 2.h7? loses, but 2.Kh5! keeps the king close to the pawn and reaches the only setup that holds. White's king stays in contact with the h-pawn, and Black can no longer make winning progress.

This is a lesson in precise defensive technique. First White uses Re3! as a shield and decoy; only then does Kh5! secure the draw. In advanced rook endings, the best defense may involve giving up material to reach the one position the stronger side cannot win.

title: Triangulation Before the Reserve Tempo
lesson_text: In this five-piece pawn ending, White is winning, but only with the precise first move 1.Ka5!. The tempting reserve move 1.a5? throws the win away, because it fixes the a-pawn too early and leaves Black with enough waiting room to hold. White must first use the king to lose a move: after 1.Ka5! Kb8 2.Kb4! Ka7 3.Kb5, the same blockade has been reached with Black to move. That is the zugzwang White needs. Black must step to a8 or b8, and White can then start improving through the c-file with moves such as Kc4 and Kd4 while the c6-pawn keeps Black tied down. The a4-pawn is still useful as a reserve resource later, but it is not the first lever. The lesson is that reserve tempi only work when the king has already created the right move order; spending the spare pawn move too early turns a win into a draw.

title: The King's Support: Reinforcing the Passed Pawn
lesson_text: In this advanced rook-and-pawn endgame, White wins by improving the king before relying on the passed f-pawn. The strongest plan begins with 1.Kg4!, bringing the king closer to the pawn and adding direct support. The pawn on f5 is attacked by Black's rook on c5 and defended by White's rook on h5, so White should not treat the pawn as already safe. The key idea is coordination: the rook protects the pawn from the side, while the king moves closer so the pawn can advance under stronger support. Black has no forced drawing defense if White plays accurately, but White must be careful because some passive moves from the starting position can let the win slip away. This does not mean a move like Rh1 is always bad; it only means that playing it immediately in this exact position gives up the win. In rook endgames, the value of a rook move depends heavily on the exact placement of both kings, both rooks, and the passed pawn. White's winning method is to improve the king first, keep the rook active, and advance the f-pawn only when the pieces are coordinated. Once the pawn reaches promotion safely, White will have enough material to force mate.

title: Distant Opposition: Recognizing the Drawn Boundary
lesson_text: This position is not a forced mate; the tablebase gives it as a draw. White can try the natural 1.Ke2, stepping toward the pawn and creating distant opposition, but that does not force the black king away from the promotion square. Black holds by staying in front of the pawn and taking the correct opposition when the kings approach, for example 1.Ke2 Ke7 2.Kd3 Kd7!. The original warning about 1.Kd1 is also inaccurate, because 1.Kd1 draws as well; in fact, every legal first move for White keeps the tablebase draw. The practical lesson is to recognize the boundary between a promising extra pawn and a won king-and-pawn ending: without forcing the defending king off the e-file at the right moment, White cannot reach the decisive sixth-rank position.

title: The Light-Squared Trap: Finishing the Bishop and Knight Mate
lesson_text: This is the final net of a bishop-and-knight mate, with the black king already driven into the correct light-squared corner for the bishop on f5. The fastest move is 1.Kb6!, tightening the box and leaving Black only 1...Kb8. White then uses the knight to force the king back into the corner: 2.Na6+! Ka8. The finish is 3.Be4#, where the bishop delivers mate on the long diagonal, the knight on a6 covers b8 and c7, and the white king on b6 seals the nearby escape squares. The important idea is that the king makes the mating net possible before the minor pieces strike; without that restriction, the black king could slip away from the corner. In this final position there is no need for a long maneuver or a 50-move-rule warning: accurate coordination produces mate in five plies.

title: The Accelerated Coronation
lesson_text: White is already winning, but the fastest tablebase route is the direct pawn break 1.c7!. This move gives Black only two king moves, and the best resistance is 1...Kxa6, capturing the decoy pawn on a6. White must then promote accurately with 2.c8=Q+!; underpromoting to a bishop or knight only draws, while a rook wins more slowly. After 2...Ka7, the queen finishes by cutting off the a-file squares: 3.Qd7+ Ka6 4.Qe7 Ka5 5.Qa7#. The white king on c5 is essential throughout, because it controls b5, b6, c6, and d6, preventing the black king from escaping toward the center. The lesson is that when one pawn can promote with tempo, speed matters more than further king improvement: the a-pawn serves as the decoy, the c-pawn crowns, and queen-plus-king coordination delivers the mate.

title: The Reserve Tempo: Precision Pawn Breakthroughs
lesson_text: In this seven-piece pawn endgame, White is winning, but the move order is extremely precise. The first move is 1.Ka3!, stepping out of the b-pawn's way while keeping the Black king trapped. Black's king on a1 has only one legal reply: 1...Kb1. Now White must use the reserve resource at once with 2.b4!. Because the b-pawn was still on its starting square, White can advance it two squares and break the locked a5/c5 pawn wall before the Black king escapes. The tempting 2.b3? is not a winning reserve tempo; it lets Black draw with 2...Kc2!. After 2.b4!, Black is lost whether the king moves or a pawn captures on b4. If 2...axb4+ or 2...cxb4+, White replies 3.Kb3!, and the remaining outside pawn push, a5 or c5, creates the decisive passer. The lesson is that a reserve tempo only works when it preserves the exact breakthrough timing; spending it one square too soon gives the defender enough time to hold.

title: Strategic Invasion: The King's Path to Promotion
lesson_text: White is winning because the centralized king can invade faster than Black can organize counterplay. The tablebase's fastest first move is 1.Ke5!, aiming for d6 and attacking the base of Black's pawn chain. This move is not the only win, but it is the cleanest route: after a best defense such as 1...Kb8 2.Kd6 Kc8, White plays 3.Kxc6 and removes the key blocker. The important correction is that White usually does not need to win the b5-pawn or promote the b-pawn first. In the shortest tablebase line, the captured c6-pawn turns White's c5-pawn into the runner: 3...Kd8 4.Kb7 Kd7 5.c6+ Kd6 6.c7 Kd5 7.c8=Q. Once the queen appears, White converts the queen-and-king ending into forced mate. The lesson is that active king invasion wins by eliminating the pawn that blocks your passer, then promoting the pawn that is already farthest advanced.

title: The Art of the Shadow: Precise King Opposition
lesson_text: In this symmetrical six-piece endgame, the pawns on a4, c4, a5, and c5 are completely locked, so the result depends on the kings alone. White's objective is to stop the Black king from infiltrating toward the base of the pawn chain. The tablebase confirms that the position is drawn, and 1.Kd3! is the clearest practical drawing move because it keeps the king central and ready to answer Black's route with the correct corresponding square. White is not limited to one move: 1.Kd2 and 1.Kc2 also hold the draw. The losing mistakes are 1.Kb2? and 1.Kb3?, which let Black take over the queenside opposition with ...Kb6 or ...Kb7 and eventually reach the pawns. After 1.Kd3, White must still shadow accurately. If Black plays 1...Kd6, White should stay in the central drawing zone with a move such as 2.Ke4, 2.Kd2, or 2.Ke2. If Black drifts away with 1...Kb6?, White is no longer in danger; active central moves can even turn the tables, while 2.Kc3 or 2.Kc2 are enough to preserve the draw. The lesson is not automatic mirroring, but corresponding-square defense: keep the king flexible, meet each Black king route with the matching barrier square, and do not give Black a free entry point into the locked chain.

title: The Mirror Dance: Mastering Corresponding Squares
lesson_text: This tablebase draw is a genuine corresponding-squares exercise, but White has very little freedom. From d2, only 1.Ke3! and 1.Ke2! hold the draw. The most active teaching move is 1.Ke3!, keeping the king close enough to answer Black's kingside probes without allowing an outflank. If Black plays 1...Kf5, White must respond with 2.Kf3!; stepping back to d3, e2, or f2 loses the opposition. If Black retreats to e7 or f7, White stays on the e- and f-files and avoids drifting toward the queenside, where the fixed b4 and d4 pawns can become targets. The tempting moves 1.Kd3?, 1.Kc3?, 1.Kc2?, 1.Kd1?, and 1.Ke1? all lose because they surrender the matching square and let Black's king penetrate. The lesson is that corresponding squares are not just about copying the opponent; they are about occupying the exact square that keeps every entry route closed.

title: Smothered Mate Mechanics: Double Check and Decoy Sacrifice
lesson_text: This is a forced smothered-mate pattern, and the tablebase confirms that 1.Nh6+! is the fastest thematic move. The knight leaves f7, opening the queen's diagonal from c4 to g8 while also checking the king from h6, so Black faces a double check and has only 1...Kh8. White then plays the decoy sacrifice 2.Qg8+!, forcing 2...Rxg8 because the rook on f8 is the only piece that can capture the queen. Once the rook lands on g8, it blocks its own king while the pawns on g7 and h7 remove the remaining escape squares. The final move is 3.Nf7#, with the knight returning to f7 to check h8. The precision point is important: many quiet queen or king moves only draw, while 1.Nh6+! keeps the entire sequence forcing from the first move.

title: Technical Proficiency: The Queen versus Rook Endgame
lesson_text: White is winning, but the first move is absolutely precise: 1.c8=Q!. The tablebase shows that underpromoting to a rook, bishop, or knight only draws, and every king move also throws away the win. After the queen appears, Black's toughest defense is to keep the rook active, with moves such as 1...Rd2 or 1...Rc1. White should not treat this as a simple king march; the conversion depends on coordinated queen checks and timely king support. One optimal tablebase route begins 1.c8=Q Rd2 2.Qc4! Rc2 3.Qe4 Kb2 4.Qb4+ Ka1 5.Qe1+ Kb2 6.Kb5, gradually driving the black king and rook into awkward alignment. In the main line, White eventually wins the rook with Qxa7 and then converts queen versus lone king to mate. The lesson is that queen-versus-rook technique starts before the queen appears: choose the only winning promotion, keep the queen active, and use checks to separate the rook from its king.

title: Troitsky Line: Blockade with the Knight
lesson_text: This rare expert endgame comes from the Troitsky-line idea: two knights cannot force mate against a bare king, but the defender's pawn can remove stalemate defenses if it is blockaded correctly. Black has the two knights, and the winning move is 1...Ne4!. The point is not just to centralize a knight; Black is preparing to meet 2.c4 with 2...Nc5!, stopping the pawn with a knight on the Troitsky line. A tempting king blockade is less reliable because the pawn can advance at the wrong moment and the defender may escape into a drawn zone. Once the knight controls the pawn, Black can use the king and the other knight to drive White's king toward a mating corner while the pawn supplies spare moves when stalemate would otherwise appear. The practical warning is serious: tablebase says the position is winning, but the conversion is long and exact. First secure the knight blockade, then begin the mating net.
